questions.prompt: A 72-year-old woman presents after her husband noticed she wasn't eating food from the right side of her plate. She says, "It's the strangest thing. When I look right at your nose I can see it clearly, but your right ear and the whole wall behind you are just... gone."
questions.description: Left calcarine fissure; contralateral homonymous hemianopsia with macular sparing.

questions.prompt: A 58-year-old, strongly right-handed accountant is brought in by his wife due to recent, puzzling difficulties at work. He reports being unable to perform simple calculations, a task he has done for 30 years, and his handwriting has become nearly illegible. In your office, when you ask him to touch his left thumb, he looks at his hands in confusion and cannot seem to tell right from left or isolate the correct finger.
questions.description: Gerstmann syndrome, localizing to dominant angular gyrus.

questions.prompt: A 65-year-old man is brought to the emergency department in distress. His wife reports that about an hour ago, his left arm and, to a lesser extent, his left leg began to suddenly flail with wild, violent, and non-stop throwing-like motions.
questions.description: Hemiballismus, most classically caused by infarcts of the subthalamic nucleus (indirect pathway); putamen may also be implicated.

questions.prompt: A 55-year-old man with a history of hypertension presents to the ER complaining of sudden, severe dizziness, difficulty swallowing, and a hoarse voice. He feels clumsy and keeps falling to his right. His right eyelid is slightly droopy and his right pupil is smaller than the left. He has lost pain and temperature sensation on the right side of his face and on the left side of his body.

A 64-year-old right-handed woman is brought to the emergency department by her husband. He states that two hours ago, while they were gardening, she suddenly seemed confused. For the last two hours, she has been calmly but repeatedly asking "Where are we?" and "How did we get here?", forgetting his answers within a minute. She knows her name and can recount detailed stories from her childhood, but she cannot recall having breakfast this morning. When you ask her to remember three words, she cannot recall any of them 60 seconds later. Exam otherwise normal.
questions.description: Transient global amnesia, most commonly localizing to left hippocampus when lesions are identified, but can be right-sided or bilateral.
